Empires & Puzzles Shale Review: The Ninja of Flying Vigor

Shale is a Ninja with a serious identity crisis. He's got the cool mask, the shurikens, and the Charge mechanic... but someone forgot to tell him that healers are supposed to heal *other people*. In this review, we break down why this Fire Ninja is often considered the 'black sheep' of the family and if there's any reason to dust him off in the modern meta.
Shale Review: The Ninja Who Forgot to Heal His Team
Shale: The Ninja Who Heals... Himself?

Shale is a 4-star Fire hero from the Ninja Tower event, released back in the wild days of 2020. As a member of the Ninja family, he boasts the unique "Charge" mana speed, allowing him to fire his special skill at 100% (5 tiles), 200% (10 tiles), or 300% (15 tiles) mana. He was designed as a flexible healer and cleanser, but he has a notorious reputation in the community for being... well, a bit selfish. While his Ninja brethren are out there slicing enemies and bypassing defenses, Shale is often found in the corner, carefully applying band-aids to himself while his team burns.

Shale sports the classic "Ninja of the Fire Nation" look. With his orange gi, face mask, and dual shurikens, he definitely looks the part. He’s got that "I’m about to do something cool" pose, which is ironic considering his special skill often involves him just standing there glowing while his teammates get pummeled. It’s a solid design that fits the Ninja Tower theme perfectly, even if his actual performance doesn't quite match the lethality of his outfit.
Shale's stats tell the story of a hero from a different era.
This is incredibly low by modern standards. Even many modern 3-star heroes hit harder than this.
Also quite low. He relies heavily on his health pool to survive.

This is his one saving grace. His base health is surprisingly high, comparable to some older 5-star heroes. This massive HP pool allows him to sponge up some damage, which is good because his defense stat won't stop much.
Compared to modern 4-star "Construct" or "Toon" heroes who regularly sport 800+ Attack and Defense with similar Health, Shale is statistically behind the curve.

Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Ninja family bonus is arguably the best part of his kit.

is fantastic. This makes Shale deceptively tanky. He might dodge a sniper shot that would otherwise kill him, or reflect damage back. If you run him with other Ninjas (even 4-stars like Sapphire or Ametrine), this bonus becomes quite consistent.
Shale does not have innate passives beyond the family bonus in his base form.
This fits his "survivalist" theme. Since his job is to stay alive long enough to heal, taking less damage helps. It's a decent defensive perk, though it doesn't solve his offensive shortcomings.
Upon release, Shale was immediately overshadowed. Players compared him to
, all of whom offered full-team support at reliable speeds. The "selfish" x1 charge was widely mocked.

The game has moved towards faster, harder-hitting heroes. 4-star tournaments are dominated by Toons and fast event heroes. A healer who needs 15 tiles to heal the whole team is simply too slow, and a healer who heals only themselves at 5 tiles is a wasted slot.

Shale is a niche hero at best. He is arguably one of the weakest 4-star heroes in the game when compared to the utility of his peers. He sits firmly on the bench for most players unless they are severely lacking in Red healers.
If you insist on using Shale (perhaps for the Ninja Tower event itself or because you love the art), you need to keep him alive.
Maximizes his high base HP and patches up his low Defense. This gives him the best chance to survive until he reaches x2 or x3 charge.
His tile damage will be negligible.
. His attack stat is so low that boosting it won't make his tiles matter much, and his special does no damage. His only job is to live and cleanse.
Can cleanse himself very quickly (5 tiles) to shake off silence or mana blocks, ensuring he can keep charging.
Can survive hits that would kill flimsier 4-stars.
The dodge/counter chance is a legitimate lifesaver and can turn tides purely on RNG.

The x1 charge is virtually useless for team support.
Heals only 3 targets at Average speed (10 tiles), whereas standard healers hit everyone.
Reaching x3 charge (15 tiles) takes too long in modern fast-paced metas.
Low defense makes him squishy despite his high HP.
Unlike Boldtusk (Attack Up) or C. Rigard (Attack Up), Shale brings zero offensive utility to the team.
The blessings in the tower allow you to pick "Mana on 5 turns" or "Mana on cast," which can make his x2 and x3 charges much easier to reach.

He is a pure healer. In Bloody Battle, he is a paperweight.
If you desperately need a Red cleanser and don't have C. Kelile or others, he is a "better than nothing" option.
. Here, Shale acts as a backup cleanser/healer to support Boldtusk. If Boldtusk dies, Shale can try to sustain the survivors.

Since his heal is a HoT (Heal over Time), any dispeller like
can wipe away his hard-earned healing instantly.
Heroes who steal healing turn Shale into a liability.
If Shale is your first 4-star Red healer, level him up, but stop as soon as you get
. Boldtusk is superior in almost every way (heals all, buffs attack, durable). Do not give Shale Limit Break materials unless you are swimming in them.

Shale is effectively a collector's item or a "Project" hero for when you are bored. There is almost no competitive reason to use him over the myriad of other 4-star options available today. Save your resources for the Toon versions of Classic heroes.
The community has never been kind to Shale. From his release, he was identified as the weak link of the Ninja family.
"I wasted mats on him. He’s useless... tough to say what he’s meant to be. An unloved child of SG."
"Just let him heal all team at every level of charge. That’s it."
"He sits at the bottom of the barrel of red heroes for me."
The consensus is clear: without a buff to make his healing hit all allies at x1 or x2 charge, he remains one of the most regrettable ascensions for many players.

Shale is a cool concept that got lost in execution. The idea of a flexible ninja healer sounds great on paper, but in practice, a healer who doesn't heal his team until he's fully charged is just a liability. He's like that one friend who brings a first-aid kit to a party but only uses the band-aids on himself. Unless you are a die-hard Ninja Family completionist, leave this stone unturned.
(Unless you really, really need a cleanser and have literally no one else).

A deep dive review of Shale, the 4-star Fire Ninja hero. Is his flexible Charge mechanic and healing enough to compete with modern healers like Costume Rigard or the new Toons? We analyze his stats, skills, and best uses.
